text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Marlena Okonkwo
Attending: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine follow-up for metastatic triple-negative breast cancer on olaparib. Patient presents for 6-month on-treatment assessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-year-old (turning 40) woman with stage IV triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), originally diagnosed with stage IIB left breast IDC, ER/PR/HER2-negative, grade 3, Ki-67 85%, in the setting of a confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.3750G>A, p.E1250*). She completed neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (Keytruda) plus dose-dense AC x4 cycles followed by weekly Taxol x12 weeks, then underwent left lumpectomy with ALND (ypT1c ypN0(i+), RCB-II), followed by whole breast radiation with regional nodal irradiation, and then adjuvant capecitabine (Xeloda) x6 cycles plus pembrolizumab maintenance to complete 17 total cycles. Approximately 18 months after surgery, she developed metastatic recurrence with involvement of the right 5th rib, T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing, and two right hepatic lobe lesions. Biopsy of the right iliac wing confirmed metastatic TNBC. NGS on the metastatic specimen revealed concordant primary alterations (BRCA1 p.E1250*, TP53 p.R248W, PTEN loss, RB1 p.Q670*) as well as acquired alterations including PIK3CA p.H1047R and CDK4 amplification. HRD score 61, TMB 11.4 mut/Mb. PD-L1 CPS on metastatic biopsy was 8 (below the threshold of 10 for pembrolizumab rechallenge in the metastatic first-line setting).

Given her germline BRCA1 mutation and adequate prior platinum-free interval, she was initiated on olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID as first-line treatment for metastatic disease, with concurrent zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q4 weeks for bone-modifying therapy. Palliative radiation to the right 5th rib (20 Gy in 5 fractions) was completed prior to olaparib initiation for pain control with good effect (pain improved from 5/10 to 1/10).

She has now been on olaparib for approximately 6 months. Today she presents for routine follow-up and response assessment. She reports grade 1 fatigue that is manageable -- she continues to work part-time and maintains her activities of daily living. She endorses mild nausea, particularly in the mornings; she has been using ondansetron PRN with good relief (taking it approximately 3-4 times per week). She denies vomiting, diarrhea, dyspnea, chest pain, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, or new bone pain. The previously treated right rib pain is essentially resolved. She reports no new neurologic symptoms. She notes ongoing residual bilateral foot tingling (grade 1 neuropathy, unchanged from prior visits, attributed to prior paclitaxel), which has not worsened. Bowel and bladder function normal.

Of note, she also reports she has been scheduled with gynecologic oncology for a risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) given her BRCA1 mutation; she is amenable to proceeding and is aware that this should be planned around her systemic therapy schedule.

She also shares that her older sister recently tested positive for the familial BRCA1 mutation and has been referred for high-risk counseling.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: mild fatigue (grade 1, stable), mild intermittent nausea (grade 1, ondansetron-responsive), bilateral foot paresthesias (grade 1, stable). Negative: fever, chills, night sweats, new bone pain or back pain, shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, new headaches, visual changes, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting (other than above), jaundice, change in bowel habits, hematuria, skin rash, oral ulcers, joint pain, or lymphadenopathy.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
- Olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID -- continuous
- Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q4 weeks (last dose 3 weeks ago, next due in 1 week)
- Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily (pembrolizumab-associated hypothyroidism, stable)
- Calcium carbonate 600 mg PO BID
- Vitamin D3 800 IU PO daily
- Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 60 mg PO daily (chemotherapy-induced neuropathy)
- Ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN nausea (olaparib-related)
- Ibuprofen 400 mg PO PRN (no longer using regularly now that rib pain resolved)
- Albuterol MDI PRN (mild intermittent asthma, rarely used)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 16, Temp 36.8C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 67.2 kg (stable from last visit).
General: Well-appearing, pleasant woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Oral mucosa moist and without ulceration or thrush. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular chains.
Chest/Breast: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no dullness to percussion, no wheezing or crackles. Left breast: well-healed lumpectomy scar at 10 o'clock position, post-radiation skin changes with mild hyperpigmentation and subtle architectural distortion, no palpable mass or skin nodule. Right breast: no masses, no skin changes. No nipple discharge bilaterally.
Axillae: No palpable axillary adenopathy bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No palpable masses.
Musculoskeletal: No midline spinal tenderness on palpation at T10. Right posterior chest wall: non-tender to palpation at the site of prior rib radiation. Right iliac crest region: non-tender to palpation. No lower extremity edema.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Sensation diminished to light touch in the bilateral feet in a stocking distribution, grade 1. No ataxia, no focal deficits.
Skin: No rashes, no purpura. Scalp hair fully regrown to pre-treatment length (patient notes this with satisfaction). No jaundice.

---

LAB RESULTS
CBC: WBC 5.2 x10^9/L (within normal limits), Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (low; Grade 1 anemia), Hematocrit 30.8%, MCV 98 fL (macrocytic), MCH 32 pg, Platelets 218 x10^9/L.
Reticulocyte count: 1.1% (low-normal for degree of anemia).
Iron studies: Serum iron 74 mcg/dL, ferritin 68 ng/mL, TIBC 290 mcg/dL, transferrin saturation 24% -- iron stores adequate, no iron deficiency.
B12/folate: Normal (vitamin B12 within normal limits, folate normal, reviewed given macrocytosis; consistent with PARP inhibitor-related effect on erythroid precursors rather than nutritional deficiency).
CMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 101, CO2 26, BUN 14, Cr 0.82 mg/dL, glucose 92. AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, Alk Phos 78 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, albumin 4.1 g/dL. Hepatic function normal.
TSH: 2.9 mIU/L (normal on current levothyroxine dose).
CA 27-29: Not routinely drawn per institutional guideline; not ordered this visit.
LDH: 198 U/L (within normal limits).

---

IMAGING
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and bone scan performed approximately 3 months after olaparib initiation (reviewed today as context for current visit): The two hepatic lesions in the right lobe showed a nice response -- the larger lesion shrunk from 1.2 cm down to about 0.9 cm and the smaller one from 0.8 to 0.6 cm. No new liver lesions. Right 5th rib shows post-radiation changes, stable. The T10 and right iliac wing osseous foci were stable on bone scan without new sites of uptake. The liver response and stable bone disease were considered a partial response to therapy. Updated restaging imaging (3-month interval since last scan) is planned for today's visit interval; results are pending and will be reviewed at next visit or sooner if available.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER (ER-/PR-/HER2-negative, germline BRCA1 p.E1250*; bone and liver involvement): Currently on olaparib 300 mg BID, 6 months of therapy. Most recent imaging (3 months ago) showed partial response with hepatic lesion shrinkage and stable bone disease. Patient clinically stable with no new symptoms, no new bone pain, no evidence of hepatic decompensation. Will proceed with interval restaging CT CAP and bone scan; results to be reviewed within the week. If ongoing response confirmed, continue olaparib at current dose without modification. Discussed with patient that median PFS on olaparib in germline BRCA-mutated MBC from the OlympiAD data is in the range of ~7 months, but responders can have prolonged benefit. We discussed monitoring for disease progression and will revisit next-line options (including platinum-based chemotherapy given HRD positivity, or consideration of sacituzumab govitecan [Trodelvy] given TNBC) when the time comes.

2. OLAPARIB-ASSOCIATED ANEMIA (Grade 1): Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, macrocytic pattern, reticulocyte count low-normal, iron stores adequate. This is a well-recognized class effect of PARP inhibitors; no intervention required at this time. Standard dose reduction threshold is Hgb < 8 g/dL or symptomatic anemia; patient is currently asymptomatic aside from mild fatigue. Will recheck CBC in 6 weeks. No supplemental iron or erythropoiesis-stimulating agent indicated at this time. Patient counseled on signs/symptoms of significant anemia to report (dyspnea, chest pain, profound fatigue).

3. OLAPARIB-ASSOCIATED NAUSEA (Grade 1): Managed with ondansetron PRN, acceptable control. Recommend taking olaparib with food to further minimize GI side effects. Continue current management. No dose reduction warranted.

4. BONE METASTASES / BONE-MODIFYING THERAPY: Zoledronic acid (Zometa) 4 mg IV q4 weeks continued; next dose in approximately 1 week. Patient on adequate calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Creatinine stable; no renal dose adjustment needed. Dental evaluation was completed prior to initiation; remind patient to avoid elective dental procedures involving jawbone and to report any jaw pain or swelling (osteonecrosis of the jaw precaution). No current ONJ concerns.

5. PEMBROLIZUMAB-ASSOCIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM: TSH 2.9, well-controlled on levothyroxine 50 mcg daily. Continue current dose, recheck TSH in 6 months.

6. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (Grade 1, bilateral feet, residual from paclitaxel): Stable, not worsening. Continues duloxetine 60 mg daily with partial benefit. No additional intervention at this time.

7. RISK-REDUCING BSO (BRCA1 germline mutation): Patient has been scheduled with gynecologic oncology for RRSO. Discussed the importance of this intervention for ovarian cancer risk reduction given BRCA1 mutation (lifetime ovarian cancer risk ~44%). She is motivated to proceed. Will coordinate timing with current systemic therapy; brief hold of olaparib perioperatively will be discussed with gyn onc team. Recommend proceeding within 2-3 months if disease is stable.

8. FAMILY GENETIC COUNSELING: Patient's sister tested positive for BRCA1 and has been referred to high-risk program. Second sister has also been encouraged to pursue testing. Commended patient for proactive engagement with cascade testing.

Return to clinic in 6 weeks for CBC recheck and restaging scan review. Sooner if any new symptoms (increased bone pain, jaundice, dyspnea, fever). Patient verbalized understanding of plan and agreement. Contact information provided for triage nurse line.

Time spent: 45 minutes, more than 50% of which involved counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
